prompt: Set up a District Materials Commerce & Logistics Portal that enables a regional education organization to plan textbook demand by course section, fulfill orders, and manage after-sales service entirely on an on-prem network. Users sign in as Students (browse and request materials), Instructors/Coordinators (generate order plans per course/class and approve exceptions), Distribution Clerks (pick/pack/issue and record custody), Moderators (manage community content), and Administrators/Operations Analysts (configure policies and monitor KPIs). The HTMX-driven interface provides fast, form-first workflows with immediate inline validation and status feedback: students can search materials, add favorites into named lists with private/public visibility, resume from browsing history, and generate shareable links that respect permissions and can expire (default 7 days). Each textbook can be rated once per student (1–5 stars) and commented with anti-spam controls (max 5 comments per 10 minutes, 500 characters, no more than 2 links), sensitive-word filtering using a locally maintained dictionary, and a reporting flow where content auto-collapses after 3 unique reports until a moderator reviews it. Ordering supports student self-selection, return/exchange requests, and a distribution ledger traceable to individuals via scan-or-type identifiers at pickup; partial fulfillment is supported through backorder splitting and reissue management for lost or damaged copies. A unified inbox surfaces system alerts and workflow messages, supports user subscriptions and do-not-disturb hours (default 9:00 PM–7:00 AM), and shows delivery/read indicators; “push” behavior is implemented as in-app banners and live inbox updates while the user is active in the browser. Operations dashboards visualize conversion, GMV, AOV, repeat purchase, and funnel drop-offs, and allow controlled exports for offline reporting. The backend uses Go with Fiber to enforce a strict order state machine (pending payment, pending shipment, in transit, completed, canceled) where “payment” is recorded as an internal receipt event (no online payments), and orders auto-close after 30 minutes in pending payment or 72 hours in pending shipment, rolling back reserved inventory immediately. Refund/return/exchange requests follow an approval workflow with hard rules (default 14-day return window, exchanges allowed only if inventory exists, refunds require a manager role), and inventory and funds adjustments are linked and auditable. SQLite is the system-of-record for orders, distribution events, messages, engagement actions, and master records; entity profiles (students, staff, courses, materials, locations) support extensible custom fields, duplicate detection (exact ID match plus fuzzy name+date-of-birth match), admin-only merge with conflict resolution, and a versioned change history capturing who/when/why. Geospatial analytics run fully offline using locally loaded boundary files and pre-packaged map tiles/layers served by Fiber; SQLite stores spatial indexes to support administrative-region aggregation, grid and buffer analysis, POI density calculations for pickup locations, and route/trajectory aggregation from recorded distribution scans, rendering efficiently even at high volume. Frontend and backend stay decoupled through REST-style endpoints returning HTML partials for HTMX swaps and JSON only where needed for charts, with local-only authentication (username/password, minimum 12 characters, lockout after 5 failures for 15 minutes), role-based authorization, encrypted at-rest storage for sensitive fields, and masking of personal identifiers in dashboards and exports by default.
